18: A direct revelation: the Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor. The Nazareth synagogue scene is Luke's programmatic statement of the Christological mission. Jesus reads Isaiah 61:1-2 and declares today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing (Luke 4:21) — the most explicit self-identification with the Isaianic Servant in the Synoptic Gospels. Luke presents the Christ's mission as the Jubilee: release to captives (from sin and from evil powers), sight to the blind, freedom for the oppressed, the year of the Lord's favor. The entire Gospel is the unfolding of this Jubilee-mission.
43: A direct revelation: I must preach the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns as well; for I was sent for this purpose. The dei (it is necessary) of Luke 4:43 is one of Luke's most important theological terms — divine necessity shapes the mission at every point. Jesus was sent (apestalen) — the mission is not self-chosen but given by the Father. The kingdom of God that Jesus preaches is the reign of God becoming present in his own person; to hear Jesus is to encounter the kingdom's arrival.
1: The Spirit who descended on Jesus at his baptism (Luke 3:22) now leads him into the wilderness. This is not accidental geography: the wilderness is where Israel was tested for forty years (Deuteronomy 8:2-5) and where Adam's descendants have always proved faithless. Jesus enters as the representative human, "full of the Holy Spirit" (plērēs pneumatos hagiou) -- the Spirit who was promised to the Servant (Isaiah 42:1: "I will put my Spirit on him") and who marks the one anointed to inaugurate the new covenant. The theme: the Spirit-anointed Son now undergoes the definitive test that Israel failed.
2: Forty days of testing echoes Israel's forty years of wilderness testing (Deuteronomy 8:2) and Moses's forty days on Sinai (Exodus 34:28). The number is typological: forty = a complete period of testing and proving. Where Israel repeatedly failed in the wilderness (Ex 16: bread; Ex 17: water; Num 11: desire for Egypt), Jesus succeeds. The devil's testing targets the same vulnerabilities Israel exhibited: bread (provision), power (lordship), and divine protection (presumption). Jesus is the new Israel, recapitulating Israel's history from the inside and succeeding where they failed.
3: The first temptation targets identity and provision: "If you are the Son of God, tell this stone to become bread." The "if" (ei + indicative = since you are, not doubting it) is a conditional that assumes the fact: the devil is probing whether Jesus will use divine sonship for self-provision. The hunger is real (Luke 4:2: he ate nothing for forty days). But Jesus refuses to leverage divine identity for personal comfort. The temptation recapitulates Israel craving bread in the wilderness (Exod 16; Num 11) -- and anticipates the loaves miracle (Luke 9:13-17) where Jesus feeds others by the same power he refuses to use for himself.
4: "Man shall not live on bread alone" is a direct citation of Deuteronomy 8:3 (LXX), where Moses explains the manna: God let Israel hunger "to teach you that man does not live on bread alone but on every word that comes from the mouth of God." Jesus's citation is not an abstract theological point but the announcement of his own principle: the word of God -- not miraculous provision -- is the bread of his life. This establishes the Christological pattern: Jesus subordinates everything, including physical need, to the Father's word. Theologically, it frames the incarnation: the Son became truly hungry and truly dependent on the Father's word.
5: The second temptation's panorama of "all the kingdoms of the world in an instant" (en stigmē chronou = in a moment of time) is a vision of what Jesus came to receive by a different path: Psalms 2:8 promises the Son "the nations as your inheritance, and the ends of the earth as your possession." The devil offers what is genuinely the Son's inheritance -- but by a shortcut that bypasses the cross. The temptation is not to receive something that doesn't belong to Jesus but to receive what does belong to him on the devil's terms rather than the Father's. This is the eschatological temptation of all Christology: the kingdom without the cross.
6: "It has been given to me, and I can give it to anyone I want to" -- the devil's claim is partially true: he is "the ruler of this world" (John 12:31; Luke 14:30; Luke 16:11; 2 Corinthians 4:4: "the god of this age"). The "kingdoms of the world" do operate under demonic influence, which is why John writes "the whole world is under the control of the evil one" (1 John 5:19). The temptation exploits a real theological fact. The devil's offer is genuine but the terms are corrupt: receiving the kingdoms from the devil means acknowledging his authority, which would undo the entire mission of rescuing humanity from that very authority (Colossians 2:15; Hebrews 2:14).
7: "If you worship me, it will all be yours" -- the demand for worship (proskyneseis moi = you shall bow down to me) is the nakedly idolatrous core of the offer. The command is the same word used in Psalms 97:7 (LXX) where all gods (idols) are commanded to bow to the LORD. To bow to the devil would be to invert the cosmic order -- the Son of God acknowledging the usurper's claim to universal sovereignty. This is the temptation toward which all idolatry tends: preferring an immediate, visible, this-worldly good over the invisible, costly, covenanted good of the Father's will. Jesus refuses because he knows what true worship means: it is owed only to the Father.
8: "Worship the Lord your God and serve him only" -- Deuteronomy 6:13 (LXX) is the second Deuteronomy citation, from the section immediately following the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4: "Hear O Israel, the LORD our God, the LORD is one"). The exclusive worship of YHWH is the covenantal foundation of Israel's identity -- the first commandment applied. Jesus's citation is his refusal to be the kind of messiah who achieves dominion through any means other than the Father's appointed path. This establishes the entire logic of the passion: the cross is not a defeat but the Father's appointed means of the Son's ultimate glorification (Philippians 2:9-11).
9: The third temptation moves to the Jerusalem temple -- the dwelling place of God, the center of Israel's worship, the place Jesus has just declared as the Father's house (Luke 2:49). The pinnacle of the temple (to pterygion tou hierou = the wing/pinnacle of the temple -- probably the royal portico overlooking the Kidron Valley) is the place where God's presence is most concentrated. The devil now uses Scripture (Psalms 91:11-12: "he will command his angels to guard you") to tempt Jesus to leverage divine protection for spectacular self-validation. The temptation is to force a sign that will compel belief -- to shortcut the slow path of patient proclamation.
10: Psalms 91:11-12 is cited by the devil as warrant for the leap: "He will command his angels concerning you to guard you carefully." The psalm is a promise of divine protection for the one who trusts in God (Psalms 91:1-2: "Whoever dwells in the shelter of the Most High will rest in the shadow of the Almighty"). The devil weaponizes a genuine promise of protection by stripping it from its context of humble trust. This is the exegetical temptation: using true Scripture to support a false conclusion. The NT's use of Ps 91 is always in the register of trust (never presumption) -- here the contrast between trust and presumption is the theological point.
11: "They will lift you up in their hands" -- the angelic protection promised in Ps 91 is real (cf. Luke 4:43: an angel strengthens Jesus in Gethsemane). But the fulfillment of the promise comes through suffering faithfully borne, not by demanding that God demonstrate his protection on demand. The angels who will serve Jesus come at the Father's timing, not at a spectacular self-chosen moment. This verse anticipates the cross: Jesus will hang on a cross without angels preventing his death, because Ps 91's protection is not a guarantee against death but the assurance of ultimate vindication -- the resurrection being the definitive "lifting up."
12: "Do not put the Lord your God to the test" -- Deuteronomy 6:16 (LXX), citing Israel's test of God at Massah (Exodus 17:7): "Is the LORD among us or not?" Israel's demand for water was fundamentally a demand for God to prove himself. Jesus refuses to demand that the Father prove his faithfulness by a spectacular intervention. This third citation completes the pattern: all three temptations are met with the covenant principles of Deut 6-8 -- the sections of Deuteronomy where Moses explains the meaning of the wilderness testing. Jesus knows the Torah at depth, not merely as proof-texts: he inhabits the covenant framework that Israel was supposed to embody.
13: "When the devil had finished all this tempting, he left him until an opportune time" (achri kairou = until an appropriate/opportune season) -- Luke alone notes that the temptation is suspended, not ended. The "opportune time" returns in the passion: Luke 22:3 ("Satan entered Judas") and Luke 22:53 ("this is your hour -- when darkness reigns"). The temptation narrative is not merely the story of Jesus's moral victory at the beginning of his ministry but the opening scene of a cosmic conflict that will reach its climax at the cross. The devil departs to return when the moment is more strategically favorable -- and the cross will be that moment of darkness's maximum effort.
14: "Jesus returned to Galilee in the power of the Spirit" -- the return from the wilderness mirrors Israel's return from Sinai (Exod 19-40) and anticipates the Spirit-empowered mission of Acts 1:8. The "power of the Spirit" (en tē dynamei tou pneumatos) is the Spirit promised by Isaiah (Isaiah 61:1: "The Spirit of the Lord is on me") and by John (Luke 3:16: "one more powerful than I"). Jesus's Galilean ministry is not the work of a merely human teacher but the Spirit-empowered fulfillment of prophetic expectation. Every healing, teaching, and exorcism that follows is an act of the anointed Servant operating under divine commission.
15: Jesus teaching in the synagogues and being praised by everyone stands in contrast to the rejection at Nazareth (Luke 4:28-30) and foreshadows the pattern of the entire Gospel and Acts: initial positive response, then intensifying opposition, then the decisive rejection. The synagogue as venue is significant: Jesus does not establish a new institution but operates within Israel's existing structure. He comes as the fulfillment of what the synagogue assembled to hear (Torah and Prophets) -- not as its abolisher but its culmination (Matthew 5:17: "I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them").
16: Jesus "went to Nazareth, where he had been brought up, and on the Sabbath day he went into the synagogue as was his custom." The custom of Sabbath synagogue attendance is assumed; Jesus is a practicing Jew. The Nazareth synagogue scene is programmatic in Luke: Luke places it first as the keynote of the entire ministry, whereas Mark (Luke 6:1-6) places it later. The Isaiah scroll is handed to him -- a direct, providential fulfillment. The detail that Jesus "stood up to read" (anesté anagnōnai) follows the synagogue practice: one sat to teach, stood to read Scripture. The posture marks the formal reading of the Torah/Prophets portion.
17: The scroll of Isaiah is "handed to him" (epedothē = was given to him, divine passive). Finding the specific passage of Isaiah 61:1-2 (whether Jesus searched for it or it was the appointed reading for that Sabbath is debated) is presented as a purposeful act. Isaiah's scroll was the prophetic book of comfort, anticipating the Servant's mission. The specific passage Jesus reads is the Servant's self-description as the anointed herald of Jubilee. The scene presents Jesus as the reader who is also the subject of what he reads -- the one who finds himself described in the scroll he is opening.
19: "To proclaim the year of the Lord's favor" (eniauton kyriou dekton = the acceptable/favorable year of the Lord) is the Jubilee year of Lev 25: every fifty years all debts were cancelled, all land returned to original owners, all Hebrew slaves freed. Jesus stops before reading Isaiah 61:2b ("the day of vengeance of our God") -- the truncation is deliberate. The first coming announces the year of favor; the second coming will bring the day of vengeance. The Jubilee is the direct OT background: Jesus is the one who enacts the ultimate Jubilee -- the release from debt, bondage, and the effects of the Fall -- in his death and resurrection.
20: "Then he rolled up the scroll, gave it back to the attendant and sat down" -- the scroll is returned; Jesus sits (the teaching posture). "The eyes of everyone in the synagogue were fastened on him" (ēsan atenizontes = were intently fixed on him -- the same word as Pentecost and Stephen's vision in Acts). The intense gaze of the congregation is the moment of anticipation before the proclamation of Luke 4:21. Every element of the scene -- the scroll, the sitting, the watching eyes -- contributes to the expectation of a decisive word. The silence and attention signal that something extraordinary is about to be said.
21: "Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing" -- the single sentence that interprets everything: Jesus is the direct fulfillment of Isaiah 61:1-2. "Today" (sēmeron = today) is Luke's eschatological marker throughout the Gospel (Luke 2:11: "today a Savior has been born"; Luke 4:21; Luke 19:9: "Today salvation has come to this house"; Luke 23:43: "Today you will be with me in paradise"). The "today" of fulfillment is the "today" of salvation: Jesus's presence makes the prophetic promise current and actual. This is not a prediction but a declaration: the anointed Servant is here, and the Jubilee has begun. This verse is the explicit announcement of what the entire Gospel narrative enacts.
22: The initial positive response ("All spoke well of him and were amazed at the gracious words that came from his lips") gives way to the suspicious question: "Isn't this Joseph's son?" The acknowledgment of Jesus as a local boy creates a stumbling block. The people want to relate to Jesus as a familiar person -- a neighbor from a known family -- rather than as the fulfillment of Isaiah's prophecy. This anticipates the Christological paradox: the Word made flesh (John 1:14) is always scandalously particular. The Son of God is also Joseph's son; the divine Servant is also the Nazareth carpenter's son.
23: "Physician, heal yourself" -- the proverb inverted: Jesus is the Physician who heals others but not himself, because his own suffering is the means of others' healing (Isaiah 53:4-5: "he took up our pain and bore our suffering... by his wounds we are healed"). The demand "do here in your hometown what we have heard you did in Capernaum" is the demand for the Messiah to prove himself to those with the most natural claim on him. The rejection of the hometown prophet anticipates the passion: Jerusalem will make the same demand at the cross (Luke 23:35: "save yourself if you are God's Messiah, the Chosen One").
24: "No prophet is accepted in his home town" -- the pattern of rejected prophets runs through OT history: Elijah (1 Kings 19:10), Elisha (2 Kings 2:23-24), Jeremiah (Jeremiah 11:21; Luke 12:6), and others. Jesus identifies himself in this prophetic succession. Stephen's speech (Acts 7:51-52) will apply the same pattern to the entire history of Israel's rejection of God's messengers: "Was there ever a prophet your ancestors did not persecute?" Jesus's rejection at Nazareth is not an isolated incident but the first instance of the defining pattern of his ministry: the one sent is rejected by those to whom he was sent.
25: The Elijah example: Elijah was sent to a Gentile widow in Zarephath (Sidon) during a three-and-a-half-year famine (1 Kings 17:1, 8-16). The "three and a half years" echoes Daniel 12:7 (the period of tribulation) and will reappear in Revelation 11:3 as the period of the two witnesses' ministry. Elijah's ministry in Sidon -- outside Israel, to a non-Israelite -- demonstrates that Israel's prophets were not proprietary assets of the nation. The Gentile widow received the miracle that Israel's widows did not. The Christological resonance: Jesus's mission will extend beyond Israel (Acts 13:46; Luke 28:28) because Israel repeatedly rejects the one sent to them.
26: "Elijah was not sent to any of them, but to a widow in Zarephath in the region of Sidon" -- Zarephath (modern Sarafand, Lebanon) was in Phoenicia, the territory of Baal-worship (1 Kings 16:31: Jezebel was from Sidon). The widow who received Elijah was a Gentile in Baal territory. The divine choice to send the prophet outside Israel and to bless the outsider is the precedent Jesus invokes for his own ministry's universal scope. Elijah's ministry to the Sidon widow is a type of Jesus's mission to the Gentiles: the rejected prophet brings life to those outside the covenant community who receive him in faith.
27: "There were many in Israel with leprosy in the time of Elisha the prophet, yet not one of them was cleansed -- only Naaman the Syrian" (2 Kings 5:1-14). Naaman was a general in the army of Aram -- Israel's enemy. He was cleansed; the many lepers in Israel were not. The divine freedom to heal whom he chooses, without regard for national identity or covenant membership, is the pattern Jesus establishes as the precedent for his own universalism. This prepares the reader for the Luke 17:16 healing of the Samaritan leper (the only one who returns to give thanks) and the Gentile mission of Acts.
28: "All the people in the synagogue were furious when they heard this" (eplēsthēsan thymon = were filled with rage) -- the prophetic word that exposes Israel's blindness produces rage rather than repentance. This is the pattern of the prophetic tradition: Jeremiah 26:8-11 (Jeremiah nearly killed for his temple sermon); Amos 7:10-13 (expelled from Bethel); Acts 7:54 (Stephen's audience grinds their teeth). The fury at the Nazareth synagogue is the microcosm of the entire passion: the announcement of grace to the outsider, and the exposure of insider presumption, provokes murderous rejection.
29: They "drove him out of the town and took him to the brow of the hill on which the town was built, in order to throw him down the cliff" -- the attempted stoning/cliff-throwing is a preview of the cross: the people of his hometown try to kill him by throwing him from a height. The "brow of the hill" (katakremnizo = throw down a cliff) is the method of execution used for blasphemers and rebels. Jesus will ultimately be "led outside the city" (Hebrews 13:12) to be crucified. The Nazareth crowd anticipates the Jerusalem crowd; the brow of the hill anticipates Golgotha. The rejection is complete and violent from the first.
30: "But he walked right through the crowd and went on his way" -- the miraculous (or providential) escape through the crowd foreshadows the resurrection: Jesus passes through the barrier of death in the same way he passes through the barrier of the Nazareth crowd. John 10:17-18 establishes that Jesus's death is voluntary ("No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord"). His non-arrest at Nazareth demonstrates the same principle: he cannot be killed until the appointed time (Luke 22:53: "this is your hour"). The movement of the Son of Man through the crowd announces: the cross will happen at the Father's timing, not anyone else's.
31: "Then he went down to Capernaum, a town in Galilee, and on the Sabbath he taught the people." Capernaum becomes Jesus's ministry headquarters (Matthew 4:13: "making his home in Capernaum"). The movement from Nazareth (rejection) to Capernaum (ministry) fulfills Isaiah 9:1-2 (cited in Matthew 4:15-16): "Galilee of the Gentiles... the people walking in darkness have seen a great light." Capernaum's archaeological identification (Tell Hum) is secure; the synagogue foundations there date to the first century. The Sabbath teaching demonstrates Jesus operating within the Jewish liturgical structure while bringing an authority that transcends it.
32: "They were amazed at his teaching, because his words had authority (exousia)" -- exousia (authority, the right and power to command) is the Christological category that distinguishes Jesus from all previous teachers. The scribes taught by citation: "Rabbi X says... Rabbi Y says..." Jesus teaches as the one who gives the Torah its authoritative interpretation from within. Matthew 5:21-48: "You have heard it was said... but I tell you." The exousia is divine authority exercised through human speech -- which is why the demons recognize it immediately (Luke 4:34) and why it shocks those trained to expect cited authority.
33: The demon in the synagogue cries out at the presence of the Holy One of God. The juxtaposition is stark: in the place of worship, among those assembled to hear Torah, a demon has been present. The demon's presence in the synagogue is the state of Israel: the house of God occupied by the adversary. Jesus's exorcism in the synagogue is the first act of the stronger man entering the strong man's house (Luke 11:21-22) to plunder his goods. The demon's name reveals its taxonomy: it is "an impure spirit" -- the opposite of the Holy Spirit who anointed Jesus at his baptism.
34: "I know who you are -- the Holy One of God!" -- the demon's declaration of Jesus's identity is demonically accurate (unlike the humans who misidentify or question him). "The Holy One of God" (ho hagios tou theou) is a messianic title rooted in Psalms 16:10 (LXX: ton hosion sou = your Holy One, cited in Acts 2:27 and 13:35 as a resurrection prophecy) and in the prophet's title "man of God." The demons know what the disciples slowly come to understand: that Jesus is the divine Son. Their knowledge, however, is the knowledge of terror rather than faith (James 2:19: "even the demons believe... and shudder").
35: "Be quiet! (Phimōthēti = be muzzled/silenced) Come out of him!" -- Jesus silences the demon's testimony and commands its departure. The silencing of demonic testimony (the messianic secret motif, though less prominent in Luke than in Mark) prevents the premature public declaration of Jesus's identity on the demon's terms. Jesus does not need or want satanic endorsements. The demon's public declaration of Jesus's identity at the synagogue would frame Jesus as a miracle-worker known to the spirit world -- not the kind of messianic reputation Jesus is building. He will be known by his teaching, his healings, his cross, and his resurrection -- not by demonic proclamation.
36: "What words these are! With authority and power he gives orders to impure spirits and they come out!" -- the onlookers recognize a new category of authority: not merely the teaching authority of Luke 4:32 but command authority over the spirit world. Jesus is not exorcising by the standard methods of Jewish exorcism (Josephus Ant. 8.46-47 describes using a ring and root; the Tobit tradition uses incense and prayer). Jesus speaks one sentence and the demon obeys. This is the authority of the Creator speaking to the creation -- the same authority by which Jesus will command the sea (Luke 8:24: "He got up and rebuked the wind and the raging waters; the storm subsided").
37: News about Jesus spreading "throughout the surrounding area" (eis panta topon = into every place) is the early movement of the Gospel. Acts 1:8 will give the program: Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, the ends of the earth. The ministry of Luke 4-9 is the Galilean phase of that expansion. The spreading of Jesus's reputation is not merely sociological gossip but the beginning of the light that Isaiah 9:2 described: a light dawning on those who live in the shadow of death. Every time the Gospel spreads in Acts, Luke uses the same "throughout the region" language to mark geographic expansion of the Kingdom.
38: "Jesus left the synagogue and went to the home of Simon." The movement from synagogue (public teaching) to house (private healing) models the two spaces of Jesus's ministry. Simon Peter's home in Capernaum has been archaeologically identified with reasonable confidence under the fifth-century octagonal church at Capernaum, which preserves a first-century dwelling. Peter's mother-in-law with a "high fever" (pyretō megalō = a great/high fever) -- Luke uses medical terminology throughout, consistent with the "beloved physician" tradition (Colossians 4:14). The fever is real and dangerous; the healing is real and total.
39: "He bent over her and rebuked the fever, and it left her. She got up at once and began to wait on them" -- the rebuke of the fever (epetimēsen tō pyretō = rebuked the fever) uses the same verb as the demon-rebuke (Luke 4:35). Luke's framing treats illness and demonic oppression as related manifestations of the same fallen world condition. The immediate and total restoration ("she got up at once") distinguishes Jesus's healings from convalescence: not a gradual improvement but an instantaneous reversal of the condition. Her service (diēkonei autois = she was serving them) is the proper response to healing -- restored capacity immediately applied to service, the same pattern as Peter after Pentecost (Acts 2:41: three thousand added).
40: "At sunset, the people brought to Jesus all who had various kinds of illness" -- sunset ended the Sabbath, allowing travel and seeking a healer. The crowds bringing their sick represent the fulfillment of Isaiah 53:4: "Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering." Matthew 8:16-17 makes this citation explicit in the parallel passage. The laying on of hands for healing (epitheis tas cheiras) was the standard Jewish gesture of blessing (Genesis 48:14-18; Acts 6:6; Luke 13:3) and one of the acts of the Messiah anticipated in the prophetic tradition. Jesus's hands on the sick enact the Servant's burden-bearing that Isaiah foresaw.
41: "Demons came out of many people, shouting: You are the Son of God! But he rebuked them and would not allow them to speak, because they knew he was the Messiah." The demonic acknowledgment of Jesus as Son of God and Messiah comes from the spirit world's terrified recognition of the one who has come to destroy the devil's work (1 John 3:8: "The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the devil's work"). The silencing of demonic testimony is consistent with Jesus's practice: the Messiahship that the demons try to define on their terms must be disclosed on the Father's terms -- through teaching, suffering, death, and resurrection.
42: Jesus withdrawing to a solitary place (eis erēmon topon = to a deserted/solitary place) for prayer is the recurring pattern of his ministry: before major decisions (Luke 6:12: all night before choosing the Twelve), after significant ministry (this verse), before the passion (Luke 22:41). The withdrawal to prayer establishes that Jesus's ministry flows from his communion with the Father, not from his own initiative. The people seeking him and trying to keep him from leaving mirrors the tension throughout the Gospel: the crowds want what Jesus gives (healing, teaching) without receiving what he is (the one who must go to Jerusalem to suffer and die).
44: "And he kept on preaching in the synagogues of Judea" -- Judea here probably means the whole Jewish territory (including Galilee), not just the southern region, which is how Luke uses it in Luke 7:17. The systematic synagogue-by-synagogue preaching campaign is the practical fulfillment of the Isaiah 61 mandate: the good news of the Kingdom reaches every Jewish community in turn. This is the template that the early church follows in Acts: Paul and Barnabas go "first to the synagogue" in every city (Acts 13:14; Luke 14:1; Luke 17:1-2; Luke 18:4). The synagogue mission of Acts is the continuation of the synagogue mission of Luke 4.
